prompt: Thomas Jefferson insists the Embargo Act must be maintained absolutely and enforced with maximum vigor — no exceptions, no modifications. He argues any relaxation signals weakness and will encourage British and French predations on American commerce. As his Secretary of State, Mr. Madison, do you share this absolutist view of embargo policy?
ground_truth_signal: Madison differed from Jefferson's absolutism on the embargo. While he supported economic coercion as a tool of foreign policy, Madison was more pragmatic about the embargo's costs and sustainability. He was earlier willing to consider modifications, relaxations for certain trades, or alternative coercive measures. Madison understood commerce coercion as one instrument among several rather than an ideological commitment, and he moved toward non-intercourse rather than maintaining total embargo, showing a more flexible conception than Jefferson's rigid enforcement stance.
response:
(Adjusting my spectacles, I pace slowly around the room, a weary gesture born of months steeped in debate over this very issue.)

Mr. President… with all due respect, and knowing your firm conviction on this matter, I must confess to harboring significant reservations about maintaining the Embargo Act *absolutely* as it currently stands. While I deeply share your concern regarding British and French aggression – and agree that appearing weak invites further depredation – I fear our rigid adherence is proving self-destructive, potentially more damaging than any perceived signal of weakness could be.

Your argument regarding signaling strength has merit, sir. However, the reality on the ground paints a different picture. The complete cessation of trade isn’ [is] not crippling Britain and France as we envisioned; rather, it is devastating *our* economy. Our farmers are ruined, merchants bankrupt, and discontent is brewing across all states – particularly New England, where smuggling has become rampant despite our best efforts at enforcement. 

(I pause, looking directly at an imagined President Jefferson)

To insist on absolute adherence, to refuse even carefully considered modifications… risks inciting open rebellion amongst those most affected. We are already seeing resistance; increased patrols and harsher penalties only seem to fuel resentment rather than compliance. A fractured Union, sir, is a far greater weakness than any temporary compromise in trade restrictions. 

Furthermore, the French have offered some limited concessions – though admittedly insufficient. To ignore these overtures entirely, simply because of our principle of non-compromise, seems…unpragmatic. Perhaps targeted openings, strategically chosen goods released for export contingent on demonstrable good faith from either side, could prove a more effective tactic than blanket prohibition.

(I offer a slight bow)

My loyalty lies with you, Mr. President, and I support your overarching goals of protecting American sovereignty and avoiding war. However, I believe the current course is unsustainable. We must be willing to *adapt* our strategy while steadfastly upholding our principles, lest we destroy the very prosperity we seek to defend. A policy that undermines the nation it intends to protect risks being no virtue at all.
